articles.title: Palestinian Death Toll In Gaza Tops 73,000 As Israel Continues Launching Strikes Despite Ceasefire
articles.content:
代尔拜拉赫，加沙地带（美联社）——加沙卫生部周日表示，以色列与哈马斯战争中的巴勒斯坦死亡人数已超过73000人，尽管自10月以来停火协议脆弱且停滞不前，以色列军事行动仍在继续。

以色列表示，其目标是哈马斯和其他构成威胁的武装分子，并对包括偶尔袭击在内的违反停火协议行为进行回应。

卫生部称，停火以来加沙已有近1000人被杀。自停火以来，五名以色列士兵阵亡。

卫生部统计数据显示，战争开始以来的死亡人数现已达到73001人。自2023年10月7日哈马斯对以色列发动袭击引发战争以来，超过173200人受伤。那次袭击造成约1200人死亡，另有251人被劫持为人质。

该卫生部隶属哈马斯领导的政府，由医疗专业人员组成，其详细记录被联合国机构和独立专家普遍认为较为可靠。该部门不区分平民和武装人员，但表示妇女和儿童约占全部死亡人数的一半。

以色列表示其努力避免伤害平民，并将平民死亡归咎于哈马斯，因为武装分子在人口密集地区活动。

**停滞的停火协议**

美国促成的10月停火协议结束了全面军事行动，促使所有剩余人质获释。然而，由于哈马斯拒绝解除武装，以色列军队在加沙推进而非撤退，协议其他条款陷入僵局。双方互相指责对方违反协议，但均表示协议仍然有效。

负责监督美国促成的加沙停火协议的首席外交官尼古拉·姆拉德诺夫表示，包括重建、以色列撤军和建立新巴勒斯坦政府在内的所有其他问题，都因解除哈马斯武装的僵局而停滞不前。

这场战争导致超过200万的巴勒斯坦人口大部分流离失所，使该地区大部分地区变成废墟，并造成食品、药品和其他基本物资的广泛短缺，因为加沙的边境口岸——几乎全部由以色列控制——被关闭。

**周末又有至少八名巴勒斯坦人被杀**

据收治伤亡人员的沙法医院卫生官员称，以色列周日下午对加沙北部贾巴利亚难民营发动袭击，造成至少4人死亡，另有数人受伤。一名按军事规定匿名发言的以色列军方官员表示，军方在该地区"打击了恐怖分子"，但未详细说明。

据巴勒斯坦卫生官员称，加沙周六夜间至周日凌晨有5名巴勒斯坦人死亡，其中包括一名13岁男孩。针对造成5人中2人死亡的周六夜间袭击，以色列军方声称其打击的是哈马斯武装分子。

马格迪从开罗报道。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

DEIR AL-BALAH, Gaza Strip (AP) — The Palestinian death toll from the Israel-Hamas war has surpassed 73,000, Gaza’s Health Ministry said Sunday, as Israeli military operations continued despite a stalled and fragile ceasefire in place since October.

Israel says it is targeting Hamas and other militants who pose a threat, and in response to ceasefire violations, including occasional attacks.

Advertisement

Nearly 1,000 people have been killed in Gaza since the start of the ceasefire, according to the health ministry. Five Israeli soldiers have been killed since the truce.

The number of deaths since the beginning of the war is now 73,001, according to the health ministry’s tally. Over 173,200 people have been wounded since the start of the war on Oct. 7, 2023, following the Hamas-led attack into Israel. That attack killed some 1,200 people and took 251 others hostage.

The health ministry, part of the Hamas-led government, is staffed by medical professionals and maintains detailed records viewed as generally reliable by United Nations agencies and independent experts. It does not distinguish between civilians and militants but says women and children make up around half of all fatalities.

Advertisement

Israel says it tries to avoid harming civilians and blames their deaths on Hamas because the militants operate in densely populated areas.

Stalled ceasefire

The U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal reached in October ended full-scale military operations and led to the return of all the remaining hostages. But other elements of the deal have stalled as Hamas has refused to disarm and Israeli troops have advanced in Gaza, rather than withdrawn. Both sides accuse the other of violating the agreement but say it is still in effect.

Advertisement

Progress on all other issues — including reconstruction, Israeli troop withdrawals and the establishment of a new Palestinian government — is being held up by the deadlock over disarming Hamas, the top diplomat overseeing the U.S.-brokered ceasefire in Gaza, Nickolay Mladenov, has said.

The war displaced most of the Palestinian population of over 2 million, left large parts of the territory in rubble and created widespread shortages of food, medicine and other basic supplies as the border crossings with Gaza — all but one controlled by Israel — slammed shut.

At least eight more Palestinians killed in the weekend

An Israeli strike on Sunday afternoon in northern Gaza’s Jabaliya refugee camp killed at least four people and wounded others, according to health officials at the Shifa hospital, where the casualties were taken. An Israeli military official, who spoke anonymously in line with military guidelines, said the military “struck terrorists” in the area, without elaborating.

Advertisement

A 13-year-old boy was among five Palestinians killed in Gaza on Saturday night and into the early hours of Sunday, according to Palestinian health officials. In response to the Saturday night strike that killed two of the five, the Israeli military claimed that it was striking Hamas militants.

___

Magdy reported from Cairo.

Related
gazaPalestine

articles.title: Tentative Deal Reached To End Iran War And Trump Orders Stop To The U.S. Naval Blockade
articles.content:
伊斯兰堡（美联社）——美国和伊朗周一凌晨达成初步协议，同意开放霍尔木兹海峡，并进一步延长不稳定的伊朗战争停火协议，这可能使急需的石油和天然气到达全球市场。

协议细节未立即公布，伊朗表示在周五于瑞士签署之前不会开始执行。这一协议可能为结束一场导致中东地区数千人丧生、包括伊朗神权政治最高领导人死亡的战争提供了途径，并引发了一场历史性能源危机。

但这份关于战争的谅解备忘录已面临严峻挑战。以色列继续与伊朗支持的黎巴嫩真主党民兵敌对行动——周日以色列轰炸了贝鲁特南部郊区——几乎使谈判破裂。

与此同时，该协议仅给出60天时间来解决伊朗高浓缩铀库存及其原子弹计划问题。这在2015年德黑兰与世界大国达成的核协议中花了数年才解决。美国总统唐纳德·特朗普在其第一任期内单方面退出该协议，为最终导致这场战争的紧张局势埋下伏笔。

“祝贺所有人！”特朗普周日在白宫举办UFC笼战庆祝其80岁生日时在社交媒体上写道。

他补充说：“我特此完全授权免费开放霍尔木兹海峡，并同时授权立即解除美国海军的封锁。”该封锁是针对伊朗控制这一关键水道的报复措施。

但他很快又有所保留，称海峡要到周五签署后才能开放。

伊朗副外长卡齐姆·加里巴巴迪在国家电视台证实了这项协议，但表示伊朗在周五签署之前不会开始执行。他说，该协议是在与卡塔尔——另一个调解方——的会谈后达成的。

以色列坚持要求在追剿真主党时拥有自由行动权，目前以色列占领着黎巴嫩南部，并将军事行动扩展到其军队25年来未曾涉足的地区。以色列尚未立即置评。以色列与美国于2月28日共同发动了这场战争。

布伦特原油基准价格下跌超过4美元，亚洲股市因此上涨。

巴基斯坦作为关键调解方宣布协议

巴基斯坦率先宣布了这一协议，总理夏巴兹·谢里夫表示“双方已宣布立即和永久停止所有前线的军事行动，包括在黎巴嫩。”目前尚不清楚以色列——虽然依赖美国但自哈马斯2023年10月7日袭击以色列以来已对敌人发动战争——是否同意该条款。

他补充说，调解人员本周将推动会议，“为技术谈判奠定基础”。

两名巴基斯坦高级官员早些时候表示，伊朗核计划等更广泛问题的谈判将在未来60天内继续进行。这些官员因未被授权公开讨论此事而要求匿名。如果双方未能在该时间内达成解决方案，时间可能会延长。

伊朗国家电视台援引最高国家安全委员会秘书处的说法称，“从今晚开始，所有前线的战争将立即和永久结束”——但美国的封锁“将立即和完全解除”。

一名知情官员表示，卡塔尔调解人员在与德黑兰进行了17小时谈判后离开。该官员因会谈的敏感性要求匿名。各方本周将在多哈分别举行预备会议。

目前尚不清楚伊朗方面谁将在周五签署协议。美国副总统JD·万斯告诉福克斯新闻，白宫仍在确定谁将出席：“我肯定计划到场，但总统本人也有可能出席。”

但美国共和党人的担忧已经显现。包括南卡罗来纳州参议员林赛·格雷厄姆在内的一些人将万斯称为“该协议的策划者”。

格雷厄姆在网上写道：“我有些担心，伊朗对协议的看法似乎与美国谈判团队声称的不同。”

纽约州众议员、众议院外交事务委员会最高民主党人格雷戈里·米克斯表示，国会将对任何与伊朗的协议进行监督。

他说：“我们一次又一次地看到：战争无法改变伊朗政权。”

临时协议面临严格审查

战争的第一波打击炸死了伊朗最高领袖阿亚图拉·阿里·哈梅内伊，其子阿亚图拉·莫吉塔巴·哈梅内伊现已成为最高领袖。自战争开始以来他一直没有公开露面，但协议的签署需要他的批准。

在宣布前的几个小时内，伊朗内部显然存在摩擦，政府警告称国内对协议的分裂会削弱其谈判地位。

该协议可能使该地区恢复到战前的状态，但伊朗已证明其有能力扰乱海峡的航运。这条水道对石油、天然气和化肥等相关产品的运输至关重要，其有效封锁震动了全球经济。

能源专家表示，即使达成协议，石油和天然气的供应也需要数月才能自由流动以满足全球需求，因为航运和保险公司需要确信协议将持续有效。

德黑兰仍然拥有弹道导弹库和足够制造数枚核武器的高浓缩铀——如果它选择追求核武器的话。

伊朗一直坚称其核计划是和平的，并未公开承诺放弃据信埋藏在三个于去年被美国轰炸严重损坏的核场地的高浓缩铀。

美国一直寻求将高浓缩铀从伊朗移除。俄罗斯已提出接收。但伊朗坚持要求保留这些铀。

——弗兰克尔发自耶路撒冷，塞维尔发自贝鲁特，韦塞尔特发自华盛顿。美联社记者梅拉妮·利德曼在以色列特拉维夫、凯西·布塞维茨在纽约、卡拉·安娜在纽约州路易斯顿对此报道亦有贡献。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

ISLAMABAD (AP) — The United States and Iran reached an initial agreement early Monday to open the Strait of Hormuz and further extend a shaky ceasefire in the Iran war, potentially allowing desperately needed oil and natural gas to reach the global market.

Details of the deal were not immediately released and Iran signaled implementation would not start until the signing, which key mediator Pakistan said would occur Friday in Switzerland. It could provide a way to end a war that killed thousands across the Middle East, including the top leaders of Iran’s theocracy, and sparked a historic energy crisis.

Advertisement

But the memorandum of understanding over the war already faced intense challenges. Israel’s continued hostilities with the Iranian-backed militia Hezbollah in Lebanon, where Israel bombed Beirut’s southern suburbs Sunday, nearly derailed the negotiations.

Meanwhile, the deal gives just 60 days to resolve what to do about Iran’s stockpile of highly enriched uranium and its atomic program. That took years to resolve in Tehran’s 2015 nuclear deal with world powers. U.S. President Donald Trump unilaterally withdrew America from that accord in his first term, setting the stage for the tensions that culminated in the war.

“Congratulations to all!” Trump wrote on social media as he celebrated his 80th birthday Sunday with a UFC cage match fight at the White House.

Advertisement

He added, “I hereby fully authorize the toll free opening of the Strait of Hormuz, and, simultaneously herewith, authorize the immediate removal of the United States Naval blockade,” which was imposed in retaliation for Iran’s grip on the crucial waterway.

He soon hedged, however, saying the strait wouldn’t open until Friday’s signing.

Iran’s deputy foreign minister, Kazem Gharibabadi, confirmed the agreement on state television but said Iran would not start implementing it until it was signed Friday. He said the deal followed talks with Qatar, another mediator.

Israel, which has insisted it be allowed a freehand to pursue Hezbollah as it occupies southern Lebanon and has extended its military operations into areas its forces haven’t been in a quarter century, did not immediately comment. Israel joined the U.S. in launching the war on Feb. 28.

Advertisement

Benchmark Brent crude oil fell more than $4 a barrel on the news as Asian stock markets rallied.

Pakistan, a key mediator, announces deal

Pakistan first announced the deal, with Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif saying “both sides have declared the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon.” It remains unclear whether Israel, which relies on the U.S. but has launched in wars against its enemies since Hamas’ Oct. 7, 2023, attack on Israel, agreed to that term.

He added that mediators this week will facilitate meetings to “lay the foundation for the technical talks.”

Broader negotiations on outstanding issues like Iran’s nuclear program would continue over the next 60 days, two senior Pakistani officials said earlier Sunday, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to discuss the matter publicly. If the sides fail to reach a resolution within that time, the timeline could be extended.

Advertisement

Iranian state television cited the secretariat of the Supreme National Security Council saying the war on all fronts “will end immediately and permanently beginning tonight” — but that the U.S. blockade “will be terminated immediately and in full.”

Qatari mediators later left Tehran following 17 hours of negotiations, said an official briefed on the developments who spoke on condition of anonymity due to sensitivity of the talks. Separate preparatory meetings with each side will take place in Doha this week, the official said.

It was not clear who from Iran would sign the deal on Friday. U.S. Vice President JD Vance told Fox News the White House was still figuring out who would attend: “I certainly plan to be there, but it’s possible the president himself could be there.”

Advertisement

But concern among Republicans in the U.S. already could be seen. They included U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, who described Vance as “the architect of the deal.”

“I am somewhat concerned that Iran’s view of the agreement seems different than what the American negotiating team is claiming,” Graham wrote online.

U.S. Rep. Gregory Meeks of New York, the top Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said Congress would exercise oversight on any accord with Iran.

Advertisement

“We have seen time and again: War cannot change the Iranian regime,” he said.

Interim deal faces intense scrutiny

The first strike of the war killed Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and Khamenei’s son, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, is now supreme leader. He has not been seen in the public since the war began, but his approval was needed for Iran to sign off on the deal.

There was apparent friction inside Iran in the hours before the announcement, as the government warned that division at home over the deal weakened its negotiating position.

The deal likely returns the region to a status that existed before the war, but with Iran having proven its ability to disrupt shipping in the strait. The waterway is crucial to significant shipments of oil, natural gas and related products like fertilizer, and its effective closure rocked the global economy.

Advertisement

Even with a deal, it will take months for oil and gas supplies to flow freely enough for the world’s needs to be met because shipping and insurance companies want to be confident the agreement will last, energy experts said.

Tehran also still has a ballistic missile arsenal and enough highly enriched uranium to build several nuclear weapons, should it choose to pursue them.

Iran has long maintained its nuclear program is peaceful and has not publicly committed to giving up the enriched uranium, which is believed to be buried under three nuclear sites that were badly damaged by U.S. strikes last year.

Advertisement

The U.S. has sought the removal of the enriched uranium from Iran as part of a deal. Russia has offered to take it. But Iran insists it wants to keep the uranium.

___

Frankel reported from Jerusalem, Sewell from Beirut and Weissert from Washington. Associated Press writers Melanie Lidman in Tel Aviv, Israel, Cathy Bussewitz in New York and Cara Anna in Lowville, New York, contributed to this report.

Related
Donald TrumpIsraelIranIran warBenjamin Netanyahu
